title: Opening Night

jokes.text: I haven't bedazzled anything since I was 22.
jokes.setup: Mother accuses David of bedazzling her portfolio
jokes.punchline: David's oddly specific denial about his bedazzling history
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - reveals David's past while maintaining his dignity through precise wording. The specificity of '22' makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, did you bedazzle my portfolio?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No. I would never bedazzle a portfolio. I bedazzle things that matter.

jokes.text: David, you were dressed like a harlequin, and you were gluing shiny jewels...
jokes.setup: Continuing the bedazzling accusation
jokes.punchline: Moira's theatrical description of David in costume bedazzling
jokes.explanation: Classic Moira vocabulary elevated by the absurd visual of David as a bedazzling harlequin.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: He emerged from that change room looking like a disco ball had relations with a funeral home.

jokes.text: perhaps that was just a night terror.
jokes.setup: Mother's detailed description of his bedazzling activity
jokes.punchline: David's calm suggestion it was a nightmare
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection - suggests the bedazzling was so absurd it must have been a dream, while dodging responsibility.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I had the most vivid dream last night that you were bedazzling a jean jacket in the living room.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That wasn't a dream.

jokes.text: Oh, of course you are.
jokes.setup: Jake introduces himself awkwardly after being caught
jokes.punchline: Moira's perfectly delivered acknowledgment of the obvious awkwardness
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy - four words that capture Moira's theatrical resignation to the awkward situation.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, well, we've established the awkwardness. It hangs in the air like a bad perm.

jokes.text: David, stop acting like a disgruntled pelican!
jokes.setup: David is flustered about the awkward situation
jokes.punchline: Moira's bizarrely specific animal comparison
jokes.explanation: Peak Moira - completely original metaphor that somehow perfectly captures David's energy. Highly quotable and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, you're being as skittish as a mink in a bathtub full of thumbtacks.

jokes.text: I see uh, David's made you feel right at home.
jokes.setup: Johnny walks in on his son with a half-dressed man
jokes.punchline: Understated sarcasm about Jake being 'at home'
jokes.explanation: Classic dad awkwardness handled with Johnny's characteristic attempt at civility despite the situation.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Oh good, you're making yourself at home.

jokes.text: In an 'afternoon delight.'
jokes.setup: Trying to explain the awkward situation to Alexis
jokes.punchline: Moira using an outdated euphemism
jokes.explanation: Moira's attempt to be delicate results in an awkwardly dated reference, perfect for her character.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, darling, it was simply a case of heavy petting, and I use that term in the most antiquated sense possible.

jokes.text: Is that term still a going thing? - No, that's no. - No, never say that again. - Don't do that.
jokes.setup: Moira's use of 'afternoon delight'
jokes.punchline: Everyone's immediate horrified reaction
jokes.explanation: Good escalation as the entire family shuts down Moira's euphemism in overlapping denial.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm going to have an afternoon delight.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Mom!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira...

jokes.text: If you take half as much care of our son as you do your physique, David should be in very good hands.
jokes.setup: Johnny trying to be welcoming to Jake
jokes.punchline: Accidentally commenting on Jake's body in front of everyone
jokes.explanation: Classic Johnny trying too hard to be cool and accidentally making it worse by complimenting Jake's body.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Jake, welcome! We're so glad you're here. You know, it's not every day we get to meet someone with such... such a substantial presence.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I mean, you really fill up a room, don't you? In the best way, of course.

jokes.text: You're like a predator!
jokes.setup: Johnny lingering during the awkward encounter
jokes.punchline: David's dramatic accusation
jokes.explanation: David's typical dramatic overreaction to his father's awkward hovering, delivered with perfect timing.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Why are you still here? Are you waiting for an apology? Because I'm not going to give you one.

jokes.text: Your next big opportunity, that's who.
jokes.setup: Johnny asking who's sitting at his desk
jokes.punchline: Bob's overly optimistic framing of someone stealing Johnny's office
jokes.explanation: Bob's cheerful spin on what's clearly bad news for Johnny - competent situational humor.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Who's sitting at my desk?

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Well, I think of it less as someone stealing your office and more as someone borrowing it without asking and never giving it back.

jokes.text: you'll know the software is working if you don't see any changes.
jokes.setup: Cal selling antivirus software over the phone
jokes.punchline: The circular logic of his scam - no changes means it's working
jokes.explanation: Brilliant scam logic - creates unfalsifiable success criteria. Smart writing that exposes the absurdity of such schemes.

dialogue.speaker: Cal
dialogue.line: Yes, hello, I'm calling to tell you that you need antivirus software. No, no, your computer hasn't shown any signs of a virus? That's because the software is working. You don't have it yet? Well, that's the problem right there.

jokes.text: He didn't even look at my computer! That's how good he is.
jokes.setup: Bob explaining how Cal fixed his computer
jokes.punchline: Bob treating obvious red flag as proof of expertise
jokes.explanation: Perfect example of Bob's gullibility - he's proud of the very thing that proves he's being scammed.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Cal fixed my computer. He said the problem was that I had too many tabs open.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: So he deleted my entire hard drive.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Now I have zero tabs open.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: The man's a genius.

jokes.text: there's uh... a cardboard box over there, you know, if you do feel like you wanna... you know, pack up your things.
jokes.setup: Johnny complaining about sharing his office
jokes.punchline: Bob's casual suggestion that Johnny just leave
jokes.explanation: Bob's oblivious cruelty delivered with casual friendliness - good cringe moment but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I don't understand why I have to share my office. This is my workspace. I need privacy, I need quiet, I need to be able to think.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Well, you could just leave.

jokes.text: Uh, first of all, who says they're sloppy, and second, who's to say we're not getting his sloppy thirds?!
jokes.setup: Alexis calling Jake 'sloppy seconds'
jokes.punchline: Stevie escalating to 'sloppy thirds' while defending Jake
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation that both defends Jake and one-ups Alexis. 'Sloppy thirds' is a great invented phrase.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Sloppy thirds.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm defending Jake.

jokes.text: Who knows how many people he's got on the go at this point?
jokes.setup: Continuing the sloppy seconds/thirds discussion
jokes.punchline: Realizing Jake might have even more people
jokes.explanation: Natural progression of the joke but doesn't add much new - competent follow-through.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Wait, how many people have you been with?

dialogue.speaker: Jake
dialogue.line: I don't know, like... a lot?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Oh my God.

jokes.text: Okay, well all I know is that someone is sloppy... and it's not me!
jokes.setup: Continuing the sloppy seconds discussion
jokes.punchline: Stevie's definitive declaration of her non-sloppiness
jokes.explanation: Nice button on the exchange but fairly straightforward callback humor.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I am not sloppy seconds. I am like... a pristine, untouched, factory-sealed item that someone just decided to return without a receipt.

jokes.text: Well, hello, everyone! What time is curtain?
jokes.setup: Moira arriving at the town hall meeting
jokes.punchline: Treating local government like theater
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moira - treating mundane small-town politics as high theater. Shows character worldview perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I must say, this town hall has all the makings of a proper theatrical production. The fluorescent lighting is positively Brechtian, and this folding chair arrangement? Pure Artaudian discomfort. I do hope someone's brought their A-game, because I certainly have.

jokes.text: If we look to have given up on ourselves, how can we assure our constituents that we haven't given up on them as well?
jokes.setup: Commenting on the council's casual dress
jokes.punchline: Treating fashion as a matter of civic duty
jokes.explanation: Moira's theatrical philosophy applied to small-town politics - brilliant character consistency and unexpected logic.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I mean, if you're going to show up to a town council meeting, the least you can do is dress appropriately. Fashion is not a frivolity—it's a civic responsibility. These people look like they got dressed in the dark at a clearance bin.

jokes.text: let the road take us where it wants. No plans, no phones, no schedules. Basically my worst nightmare.
jokes.setup: Mutt describing their spontaneous road trip
jokes.punchline: Alexis immediately identifying this as her personal hell
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Alexis's honest reaction to hippie lifestyle creates good contrast and reveals character.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: We just threw some stuff in the van and drove. No plan, no reservation, just spontaneity.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So you're describing my personal hell.

jokes.text: or the father who got stuck in a mine!
jokes.setup: Moira arguing for extended daycare hours with relatable examples
jokes.punchline: Suddenly jumping to extremely specific mining accident
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation from relatable (manicurist) to completely absurd (mine accident) - perfect Moira theatrical logic.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The children need extended hours! Some of us have important commitments. I have a dermatology appointment that simply cannot be rescheduled, and then I'm meeting with my colorist, and frankly, after the incident in the mines of Transylvania, I simply cannot be away from a licensed esthetician for more than six hours.

jokes.text: That extra half hour could be the difference between a child who gets picked up safely, and one who wanders the street, waiting for a dust-covered man to emerge from the darkness!
jokes.setup: Continuing the mining accident scenario
jokes.punchline: Vivid dramatic imagery of dust-covered miner father
jokes.explanation: Peak Moira melodrama - creates cinematic imagery for mundane daycare issue. Highly quotable theatrical language.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh my God, Johnny covered head to toe in coal dust, looking like some sort of... tragic Shakespearean figure emerging from the depths of the earth.

jokes.text: Homeless toddlers?! Tugging at your pant legs?!
jokes.setup: Continuing her dramatic daycare argument
jokes.punchline: Escalating to apocalyptic vision of homeless children
jokes.explanation: Continues the escalation perfectly - from mining accidents to homeless children tugging at legs. Pure Moira theatrical excess.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: So what you're telling me is that you're going to turn these children out onto the street? Into the cold? Homeless? Destitute? Wandering the highways like little Dickensian orphans, begging for gruel?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Is that the legacy you want? Because I can see it now — a documentary in ten years, 'Where Are They Now?' And it's just footage of your former students huddled under bridges, asking themselves, 'Why? Why did Mrs. Rose abandon us?'

jokes.text: This isn't 'Sunrise Bay,' where everybody lives in a perfect dream world!
jokes.setup: Johnny trying to ground Moira's theatrical approach
jokes.punchline: His reference to her soap opera as unrealistic
jokes.explanation: Good character moment and series callback, though the irony is fairly obvious.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, you can't just burst into rooms and deliver monologues. That's not how normal people behave.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, normal people don't have my bone structure, but here we are.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm serious. Even on that soap opera you did, 'The Crows Have Eyes III,' characters had some semblance of realism.

jokes.text: Ohhh, okay, why don't I just tiptoe into my orchard of money trees, and fill my basket with money for you!
jokes.setup: Moira demanding they find money for daycare
jokes.punchline: Ronnie's sarcastic fantasy about money trees
jokes.explanation: Good sarcastic escalation that builds into the diamond carrots bit - sets up the bigger payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: Oh sure, let me just go plant some money trees. I'll have them grow overnight, and by morning, we'll have a whole orchard of crisp hundred dollar bills just hanging there like apples.

jokes.text: And while we're at it, why don't I gather up some diamond carrots, and some golden potatoes! And I don't mean the kind of potatoes you eat, I mean, the... potatoes that are made out of actual gold.
jokes.setup: Ronnie's sarcastic money trees comment
jokes.punchline: Escalating to increasingly absurd magical vegetables with unnecessary clarification
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation with great specificity. The clarification about gold potatoes pushes it over the top absurdist.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: What, do you think money grows on trees?

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: Oh wait, it does. I planted some cash seeds last spring and I got a beautiful money tree. Plus a bonus asparagus plant that grows actual asparagus, which is weird because I planted money.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: Then I got a potato plant that grows little bags of cash instead of potatoes. Those are literal bags of money, by the way, not potato-shaped money.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: And now I've got this carrot situation where the carrots are made of hundred dollar bills. Actual carrots made of cash. Not cash-colored carrots. Carrots. Made of bills.

jokes.text: well then, just call me Johnnymichael.
jokes.setup: Amygrace correcting Johnny about her always being Amygrace
jokes.punchline: Johnny's deadpan creation of his own compound name
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and character moment - Johnny's attempt to match her pretension with understated humor.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well then, I shall be Johnrose.

jokes.text: Screamnastic inner/outer beauty connectivity.
jokes.setup: Explaining what rural communities lack
jokes.punchline: The ridiculous corporate buzzword mashup
jokes.explanation: Good satire of wellness industry buzzwords - captures the empty corporate speak perfectly.

jokes.text: Oh, we don't call them that.
jokes.setup: Johnny suggesting opening a 'Screamnasium' by Christmas
jokes.punchline: Amygrace's flat rejection of the obvious portmanteau
jokes.explanation: Good deflation moment - Johnny's reasonable assumption gets shot down with corporate precision.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We could open a Screamnasium by Christmas.

dialogue.speaker: Amygrace
dialogue.line: Absolutely not.

jokes.text: I think I'm getting a little high... from the fumes.
jokes.setup: Jake working on painting furniture
jokes.punchline: Casual admission of being high from paint fumes
jokes.explanation: Simple observational humor - competent but not particularly clever or character-specific.

jokes.text: One of my fondest memories from childhood were weekdays, between 5:30 and 6 P.M. That was our time!
jokes.setup: Moira now arguing against extended daycare hours
jokes.punchline: Claiming fond memories of a very specific 30-minute window
jokes.explanation: Great character moment - Moira's theatrical childhood memories reduced to a specific TV time slot. Shows her artificial relationship with normalcy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I have nothing but fond memories of that very specific 30-minute window between when David came home from school and when I had to pick him up from daycare.

jokes.text: You literally could not have said that any faster.
jokes.setup: Jake quickly clarifying he and Alexis aren't together
jokes.punchline: Alexis pointing out the speed of his denial
jokes.explanation: Good observation humor - captures the awkwardness of Jake's eager denial with perfect timing.

dialogue.speaker: Jake
dialogue.line: We're not together.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wow, that was fast.

jokes.text: We're picking pinecones. I got this old compass at a flea market; we're just gonna drive until we see the pine trees. I'm pretty sure the compass is broken, though.
jokes.setup: Explaining their pinecone harvest trip
jokes.punchline: Using a broken compass to navigate to pine trees
jokes.explanation: Good hippie logic satire - the casual admission of the broken compass undercuts the whole adventure.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: So I was using this broken compass to navigate out to the pine trees.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: A broken compass? How did that possibly work?

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: Well, it didn't. We ended up three miles in the wrong direction.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Of course you did.

jokes.text: Which you must really be... 'pining' for by now!
jokes.setup: Discussion about pinecone harvesting
jokes.punchline: Alexis's forced pine/pining pun
jokes.explanation: Deliberately bad pun that's meant to be awkward - works for character but not particularly clever on its own.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm not really pining for the pinecone business.

jokes.text: Stay away from the Cafe, the olives are bad.
jokes.setup: Twyla greeting Moira and others
jokes.punchline: Her casual warning about bad olives
jokes.explanation: Good setup for Johnny's condition revelation, economically delivered warning.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Hi Moira. Just a heads up - those olives by the cheese are starting to turn a little funky. I'd skip those.

jokes.text: Oh John, you smell like my mother!
jokes.setup: Johnny coming home drunk from olives
jokes.punchline: Moira's specific comparison to her mother
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - reveals something about Moira's family while addressing Johnny's condition in her unique way.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh my God, you smell like a martini factory. You're acting exactly like my mother used to.

jokes.text: Not at this exact moment perhaps, you have an olive stain on your tie.
jokes.setup: Moira giving Johnny an encouraging speech about being a good man
jokes.punchline: Immediately undercutting it with observation about his appearance
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Moira's attempt at being supportive immediately undermined by her attention to appearance. Great structure.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You are a good man, Johnny. A truly good man.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: But that shirt is doing you no favors.

jokes.text: Um yeah, I would love to order something else, but I'm just having trouble... seeing the menu. Yeah, so um... if we could somehow make it brighter in here?
jokes.setup: Johnny trying to order breakfast while hungover
jokes.punchline: Asking them to make the restaurant brighter because he can't see
jokes.explanation: Good hangover humor - Johnny's polite approach to his impaired vision creates mild cringe comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Could you... could you turn up the lights in here? I can't see anything.

dialogue.speaker: Waitress
dialogue.line: The lights are on.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, turn them up more. I'm hungover and I can't see the menu.

jokes.text: and I'll have a bowl of room temperature hollandaise sauce, please.
jokes.setup: Family ordering breakfast while Johnny is hungover
jokes.punchline: David's absurd hangover food request
jokes.explanation: Brilliant David moment - the specificity of 'room temperature' and choosing hollandaise sauce is perfectly absurd and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'd like the eggs benedict, but can you make the hollandaise with a more aggressive aioli situation, and can we get some microgreens involved? Actually, you know what, just bring me a bottle of water and some aspirin.

jokes.text: A toast!
jokes.setup: Moira declaring they should celebrate Johnny's resilience while he's clearly hungover
jokes.punchline: Suggesting a toast to someone who clearly can't handle more alcohol
jokes.explanation: Perfect irony - Moira's theatrical gesture is exactly wrong for the situation. Great economy with just two words.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'd like to propose a toast to Johnny's resilience.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: To a man who can drink an entire bottle of wine and still wake up the next morning.

jokes.text: Well I, for one think we should celebrate your resilience! A toast!
jokes.setup: Johnny insisting he's fine despite being obviously hungover
jokes.punchline: Moira's suggestion to toast his 'resilience' when alcohol was the problem
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic suggestion that shows Moira's obliviousness while making the situation worse.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I think we should toast to your resilience.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, I appreciate the sentiment, but alcohol is what got me into this mess in the first place.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Precisely. And look how well you're handling it.

tmdb_overview: Johnny struggles to find a new purpose, while Moira must confront the harsh realities of local politics.
insight: Season premiere relies on character comedy over punchlines, landing 74 with modest density.
